shower.thought: You never actually see the color you're looking at—you see the color of light that bounced off it.
If you're having a conversation with someone while they're on their phone, you're not competing with their phone for attention. You're competing with every person who's ever texted them.
The fact that we need alarm clocks means we've engineered our lives in a way that directly opposes what our bodies actually want.
You can't remember most of your life, which means you're essentially living with an unreliable narrator inside your own head.
Every photo you take is you admitting that the moment wasn't good enough to just experience—you needed proof it happened.
We joke about how our parents didn't understand our problems, but we're probably doing the exact same thing to our kids with theirs.
The more efficient we make our lives, the less time we seem to have.
You're the only person who will ever know what it feels like to be you, which means everyone's loneliness is completely unique and incommunicable.
We're terrified of making the wrong choice, but most of our lives are shaped by decisions we don't remember making.
Your body is constantly dying and being rebuilt, so technically you're never the same person who went to bed last night.
The things you worry about at 3 AM will probably seem insignificant in a week, but that doesn't stop the 3 AM version of you from suffering.
We value honesty until someone's honesty makes us uncomfortable.
You'll never know if the way you perceive colors is the same as how other people perceive them, and that's genuinely unsolvable.
Nostalgia is just your brain cherry-picking the good parts and filing away all the times you were bored or uncomfortable.
If you're good at faking confidence, nobody ever knows if you actually have it or not.
We're all just doing our best impression of what we think an adult is supposed to be.
The hardest part of being productive isn't the work—it's convincing yourself you deserve to take a break.
Every conversation ends with someone deciding when it's over, and sometimes that person's wrong about the other person being done.
You can spend decades with someone and still not fully know them, because people aren't static. They're constantly becoming different versions of themselves.
The saddest part about growing apart from people isn't the distance—it's realizing you can't get back to how things used to feel even if you wanted to.

motivational.thought: The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones. — Confucius
Comparison is the thief of joy, but progress is its reward. — Unknown
Your future self is watching you right now through memories you haven't made yet. — Unknown
The obstacle is not blocking the way. The obstacle is the way. — Marcus Aurelius
Don't wait for the perfect moment. Take the moment and make it perfect. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
The only way out is through. Everything else is just delay. — Unknown
You were not born to fit in. You were born to stand out. — Unknown
Small daily improvements are the antidote to overwhelming change. — Unknown
Your life is not measured in years, but in the moments you actually felt alive. — Unknown
The person you'll be in five years is determined by the books you read and the people you surround yourself with today. — Unknown
Regret is heavier than effort. Choose wisely. — Unknown
What seems impossible today will one day be your warm-up. — Unknown
The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now. — Chinese Proverb
You don't rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems. — Unknown
Courage is not the absence of fear. It's the decision that something else matters more. — Unknown
The quiet voice that says "try again" is louder than the noise that says "give up." — Unknown
Your potential is infinite, but your time is not. Choose accordingly. — Unknown
Every expert was once a beginner who refused to quit. — Unknown
The life you want is on the other side of the discomfort you're avoiding. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my friend he was adopted.
He said, "I know," and I realized his parents had actually wanted him.

My therapist asked if I ever think about the future.
I said not since my horoscope told me not to worry about it.

I joined a support group for people with severe anxiety.
We met in a dark alley at midnight so no one would see us.

My father said, "Son, I'm not mad, just disappointed."
I said, "Thanks for clarifying—I thought you were just my reflection."

I asked my accountant if I should be worried about my financial situation.
He said, "Only if you sleep."

My friend said suicide hotlines save lives.
I said, "Only if you answer before you jump."

I told my kid the tooth fairy wasn't real.
He said, "I know—I've been leaving you teeth and you've been stealing my money."

I have a stepladder because my real ladder left when I was young.
We don't talk about why.

My boss asked why I seemed so unmotivated.
I said, "Would you be motivated if you knew exactly how you'd die?"
He said, "No, but you're getting a write-up anyway."

I accidentally told my wife her cooking was mediocre.
Now I'm getting really good at making reservations.

My therapist says I use humor as a defense mechanism.
I said, "Better than a sword—less paperwork."

I tried to call a suicide prevention hotline.
The wait time was so long I reconsidered my entire life choices.
Turns out they weren't suicidal thoughts, just customer service anxiety.

My friend asked if I believed in ghosts.
I said, "Only the ones that haunt my bank account."

I told my wife she was driving me crazy.
She said, "That was my plan all along."

I asked my dog why he looked so depressed.
He said, "Have you met you?"

My colleague asked if I had any life goals.
I said, "Yeah, to make it to retirement without anyone realizing how little I've accomplished."

My mom asked if I was eating enough vegetables.
I said, "Define 'enough.'"
She said, "More than zero."
I said, "Well, that's going to be difficult."

I went to a couples therapy session alone.
The therapist asked where my partner was.
I said, "That's what we're trying to figure out."

My friend said he was cutting ties with toxic people.
I said, "That's great—when are you leaving?"

I asked my psychiatrist if I was getting better.
He said, "You're definitely getting something—I'm not sure it's better."
